comments.author: @telekhal
comments.text:
Lebanese speaking here. 
This is a weird documentary.
At first sight: ok, nice… Celtic music feels displaced but then, let it be…
But further on you realise: a few drone clips patched together with a generic blah text and “enriched” with some AI generated sequences: while they describe the cedars, they hardly show any but a lot of other trees… 
The sheep: OMG! You never find those sheep in Lebanon. Lebaneses sheep have no tails but “Lyyié” (Lebanese know what I mean). And then the sheep are mounted by the AI in front of a virtual, snow covered mountain… such a rubbish. 
The documentary is supposed to show the beautiful side of Lebanese mountain life, which is what it doesn’t show…
It’s beautiful to watch for a foreigner though and it’s not harmful to Lebanese reputation. 
So what….

comments.author: @Raghdaa.a.k
comments.text:
Thank you for this piece of art.
I like how you've collected the footages of all places during sunset at the end. Genius touch.

You did well with photography and storytelling, but you could do better with the cedars. Your camera couldn't really capture their glory. There are really huge cedar trees with their trunks so large it takes tens of people to be able to hug them. I wish you showed that.

But overall, it's fantastic.

comments.text: Lebanon’s mountain life honestly feels like the kind of place where time decided to slow down and enjoy the view. comments.author: @RostislavLapshin
comments.text:
06:09 The Stone of the South, the West Stone, and the recently discovered 1,650-ton megalith located near the Stone of the South were not cut out from a single piece of limestone in a quarry, but were cast in situ from a concrete-like material. During the casting process, masonries made of relatively small stone blocks were used as a rigid internal filler. A similar conclusion was drawn regarding the “trilithon” blocks and the plinth blocks forming the large podium of the Temple of Jupiter. Thus, the Baalbek megaliths were never transported from the quarry to the construction site. A number of features identified during a detailed analysis of the masonry of the Temple of Jupiter podium led to the conclusion that the so-called medieval Arab fortress was erected before the construction of the ancient Roman temple, rather than after, as is commonly believed today. The latter finding, together with the presence in the Baalbek structures of cavities from reinforcement in the form of multi-meter metal rods of large cross-sections, indicates that the “ancient Roman” buildings were erected during the era of industrial production of iron and large-sized products of it.

Details are in the article “Analysis, presumed structure, method and time of fabrication of the Baalbek trilithon and “tired” parallelepipeds” (DOI: 10.24108/preprints-3114824, 50 pp., 2026).
